mnemonics.title: Transport Layer Functions
mnemonics.content: SEND FREC: Segmentation, End-to-end, Multiplexing, Demultiplexing, Flow control, Reliability, Error control, Congestion control

mnemonics.title: TCP Flags
mnemonics.content: URGent PSH FIN SYN RST ACK - UP SFA NRA (Use Push For SYN Not Reset ACK)

mnemonics.title: TCP Three-Way Handshake
mnemonics.content: SYN -> SYN-ACK -> ACK: SYNC (Synchronize) before you TALK

acronyms.usage: Reliable, connection-oriented transport protocol for ordered data delivery
Lightweight, connectionless protocol for real-time applications
Maximum data bytes in a TCP segment, typically 1460 bytes
Time for a packet to travel to destination and back
Timer value before TCP retransmits unacknowledged data
Reliable, message-oriented protocol with multi-streaming support

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 20-byte (160-bit) divided into 5 rows: Row 1 has ports (32 bits), Row 2 has sequence numbers (32 bits), Row 3 has acknowledgment and data offset (32 bits), Row 4 has flags and window (32 bits), Row 5 has checksum and urgent pointer (32 bits). Think of it as a 5-row postal address form.

Picture a train of boxcars (segments) moving on a track. The window is a viewing window that shows which boxcars can be sent. As ACKs arrive, the window slides forward, allowing new boxcars to enter the track. The window size determines how many boxcars can be in motion simultaneously.
Think of a phone call: Person A says HELLO (SYN), Person B says HELLO BACK (SYN-ACK), Person A says YES (ACK). Only after this exchange can actual conversation (data transfer) begin.

analogies.analogy: The Network Layer is like a postal system delivering letters between cities (hosts), while the Transport Layer is like the mailroom at each building, sorting letters to specific rooms (applications/ports) within that building.
TCP is like ordering books online with tracking, delivery confirmation, and guaranteed arrival in order. UDP is like sending a postcard - it might arrive, might not, and the order doesn't matter.

Flow control is like a restaurant waiter regulating how fast the kitchen prepares dishes based on how quickly the customer is eating - the kitchen (sender) can't overwhelm the customer's table (receiver).
Congestion control is like traffic lights and roundabouts preventing road (network) collapse during rush hour - all drivers (senders) must moderate their speed to prevent gridlock.

Port numbers are like apartment numbers in a large building (IP address). The street address (IP) gets you to the building, but you need the apartment number (port) to reach the specific person (application).
